coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission biomass carbon dots for near-infrared ratiometric fluorescence determination and imaging of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Cui
Long, Ruiqing
Tong, Xia
Guo, Ying
Tong, Chaoying
Shi, Shuyun
coredata.dc:description: Ascorbic acid (AA) is essential for human body. Thus, its selective, sensitive, and accurate sensing and imaging are greatly important for clinical chemistry and diagnostics. Here, carbon dots (CDs) with two emissions at 520 and 668 nm under 415 nm excitation were effectively fabricated via solvothermal method using black pepper as raw materials. Formation of nonfluorescent CDs-Fe3+ complex led to the main quenching of near-infrared (NIR) emission at 668 nm. Then redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA recovered the fluorescence. Thus, the resultant CDs were developed as a NIR ratiometric nanoprobe for AA determination based on off-on mode with excellent selectivity and high sensitivity. In addition, the good photostability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility made as-prepared CDs a biosensing platform for intracellular AA imaging. As a novel member of dual-emission CDs, the proposed strategy has tremendous potential to broaden their applications in biological fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Black pepper
Near-infrared sensor
Ratiometric determination
Cellular imaging
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of dual-emission CDs
Mechanism of off-on ratiometric AA sensing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province , China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province , China ( 2018JJ1043 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Strategy for preparation of dual-emission CDs by black pepper and ratiometric detection of AA.
TEM image (A, insert is the size distribution), XRD (B), FT-IR (C), XPS survey spectra (D), high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (E), N 1s (F), and O 1s (G) of dual-emission CDs.
UV–vis spectrum and fluorescence spectra of dual-emission CDs (A), fluorescence spectra of dual-emission CDs under different excitation wavelengths (B).
Fluorescence spectra of dual-emission CDs after addition of different concentrations of AA (A), linear relationship between F668/F520 and the concentrations of AA (B).

Intracellular images: HeLa cells incubation with CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 1.5 mL) (A1–A3); HeLa cells incubation with CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 1.5 mL) and Fe3+ (10 μM, 1.5 mL) (B1–B3); HeLa cells incubation with CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 1.5 mL), Fe3+ (10 μM, 1.5 mL) and AA (100 μM, 1.5 mL) (C1–C3).
Concentration, recovery and RSD of AA detection in human serum samples by CDs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Dual-emission biomass carbon dots for near-infrared ratiometric fluorescence determination and imaging of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Pharmacology, Xiangya Hospital, Hunan Key Laboratory of Pharmacogenetics, Central South University, 410078 Changsha, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Pharmacology, Xiangya Hospital, Hunan Key Laboratory of Pharmacogenetics, Central South University, 410078 Changsha, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Clinical Pharmacology, Xiangya Hospital, Hunan Key Laboratory of Pharmacogenetics, Central South University, 410078 Changsha, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is essential for human body. Thus, its selective, sensitive, and accurate sensing and imaging are greatly important for clinical chemistry and diagnostics. Here, carbon dots (CDs) with two emissions at 520 and 668 nm under 415 nm excitation were effectively fabricated via solvothermal method using black pepper as raw materials. Formation of nonfluorescent CDs-Fe3+ complex led to the main quenching of near-infrared (NIR) emission at 668 nm. Then redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA recovered the fluorescence. Thus, the resultant CDs were developed as a NIR ratiometric nanoprobe for AA determination based on off-on mode with excellent selectivity and high sensitivity. In addition, the good photostability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility made as-prepared CDs a biosensing platform for intracellular AA imaging. As a novel member of dual-emission CDs, the proposed strategy has tremendous potential to broaden their applications in biological fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), an indispensable trace organic nutrient known as vitamin C, has been widely used in food additives, health care supplements, and medicines to improve human health [1]. AA has strong reducibility, which is always involved as an effective antioxidant to reduce the oxidative stress from the ascorbate peroxidase substrate in the physiological metabolism [2]. There are also many important biosynthesis processes relating to AA, such as collagen formation, tyrosine and tryptophan metabolism, iron utilization, normal development of bone dentine cells, as well as immune activity enhancement [3,4]. Deficiency of AA can cause various diseases, such as common cold, inflammation, scurvy, arteriosclerosis and cancer [5]. In contrast, an excess may cause the formation of oxalic acid and uric acid stones, diarrhea, nausea, etc. [6]. Thus, it is considerably significant and urgent to establish sensitive and selective strategies for determination of AA in biological samples. To date, a variety of analytical methods have been developed to detect AA in food, human serum samples, and living cells, such as electrochemistry [7], colorimetry [8], high performance liquid chromatography [9], and fluorescence [6,10–13]. Among the developed methods, fluorescence-based assays have been considered as the most effective method because of their simple operation, excellent sensitivity, high accuracy, non-invasiveness, real-time detection, and superior biocompatibility [10–13].

Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of popular fluorescent materials, possess outstanding properties (e.g. facile preparation procedures, superior optical properties, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility) and wide applications (e.g. sensing, imaging, photothermal therapy, drug delivery) [14–20]. It is well known that the types of raw materials (e.g. small organic compounds, biomolecules, natural products) and carbonization conditions (e.g. hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave, pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation) have effect on physicochemical functionalities and photoluminescent (PL) propertied of CDs. It is noted that natural products are rich of chemical components with abundant heteroatoms (e.g. N, P, S) [21,22], which are the ideal raw materials for fabrication of unique biomass CDs with controllable PL properties [15,20]. To date, most biomass CDs have been prepared by hydrothermal method to achieve single emission. Comparing with single-emission fluorescent sensor, dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence detection has attracted much attention because it can avoid instrumental fluctuations and background interferences by the built-in correction of reference signal, and then enhance the detection accuracy and sensitivity [23,24]. Importantly, porphyrins, derived from hydrophobic chlorophyll, can act as co-precursors to obtain intrinsic dual-emission biomass CDs with NIR ratiometric properties by solvothermal method [14,19]. Notably, NIR ratiometric fluorescence sensors were highly desired in biomedical detection and imaging due to the lower fluorescence background interferences and deeper imaging penetration [14]. Consequently, development of novel dual-emission biomass CDs by solvothermal process for NIR ratiometric fluorescence sensing and imaging of AA will greatly facilitate the AA tracking in biological samples.

Here, black pepper, one of the most widely consumed spices containing alkaloids and porphyrins [25], has been selected as starting materials for the first time to synthesize ratiomretric CDs with two emissions (520 and 668 nm) under a single wavelength excitation at 415 nm. The NIR emission peak at 668 nm can efficiently and selectively be quenched by Fe3+ mainly due to the static quenching effect (SQE, chelation between Fe3+ and porphyrins of CDs), and then recovered by exposure to AA because of the reduction of Fe3+. In contrast, green emissive peak at 520 nm had a slight overlooked change, and can be used as the reference signal. As a result, the CDs were well applied as an off-on fluorescence nanoprobe for specific ratiometric sensing of AA in human serum samples. In particular, the noninvasive bioimaging of AA in living cells signified the superiority of NIR ratiometric fluorescence sensors. Obviously, the proposed strategy provided a new sight for studying the biomedical function of AA.
All analytical chemicals presented in Supplementary materials were commercially available and used without any purification.

Tecnai G2 20S-Twin transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Prague, Czech) was selected to investigate the morphology of CDs. Zetasizer Nano S90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK) was used to measure the polydispersity and particle size of CDs. Rigaku D/max2500v/pc X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) was chosen to study the lattice of CDs. PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA) and IR Prestige-21 Fourier transforminfrared (FT-IR) (Shimadzu, Japan) were optimized to analyze the functional groups and compositions of CDs. Perkin-Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and Fluo Time 100 (PicoQuant, Germany) were determined to study the fluorescence properties and fluorescent lifetimes of CDs with excitation wavelength at 415 nm. UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was selected to record UV–vis spectra of CDs. Enzyme linked immunosorbent assay reader (BioTek Instrument Inc., Winooski, USA) was applied for methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay. Snap cool HQ2 imaging system (Photometrics Inc., USA) was opted to carry out the cell imaging.

Fig. 1 shows the preparation procedures of dual-emission CDs by black pepper. In brief, black pepper was obtained from local supermarket, which was planted in Guangxi, China. Black pepper was firstly powdered and sieved (500 mesh), and then the obtained powder (2.0 g) was immersed by anhydrous ethanol (40 mL) and refluxed at 100 °C for 24 h in nitrogen atmosphere in a round-bottom flask (100 mL). After cooling to room temperature, the solution (6.0 mL) was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane, and further refluxed with H2N-PEG-NH2 (3.4 KD, 1.2 mg/mL)/anhydrous ethanol solution (1.5 mL) at 75 °C for 4 h in a round-bottom flask (25 mL). Finally, the resultant solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24 h with renewing the water every 4 h through dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO), and then dried to obtain dual-emission CDs.

As-synthesized dual-emission CDs were mixed with phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM) to prepare CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL). The selective response of CDs to Fe3+ was firstly studied against other metal ions (Li+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Fe2+). Then CDs solution (3 mL) was incubated with Fe3+ solution (3 µL, 3 mM) for 15 min at room temperature to prepare CDs-Fe3+ probe. After that, AA at different concentrations were added, and fluorescence spectra were recorded after 415 nm excitation after another incubation for 20 min at room temperature. The ratio values of fluorescence intensities at 668 and 520 nm (F668/F520) were calculated for ratiometric detection of AA. The selectivity of CDs-Fe3+ probe to AA response was investigated against small biomolecules (Hcy, GSH, Cys, Tyr, Leu, Ala, Trp, Thr, Lys, Ile, Met, Ser, Val, DA, CoA).

Human serum sample was collected with the appropriate Ethical Permissions (approval number: 2018111099) and Informed Consents at Xiangya Hospital of Central South University, Changsha, China. Human serum was firstly diluted 10-fold using phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM). Then the diluted human sample was added with different concentrations of AA, which were mixed with CDs-Fe3+ probe for fluorescence spectra analysis.

Cell viability assessment of CDs was evaluated by MTT assay. Specially, HeLa cells (CCL-2 in ATCC, 1 × 104 per well) were seeded in 96-well plate and attached for 24 h. After removal of cell culture medium, different concentrations of CDs (0–1 mg/mL) were added and incubated for another 24 h. Next, PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM) was used to rinse the cell, and then freshly prepared MTT (150 µL, 0.5 mg/mL) was added to incubate for 4 h. Finally, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was introduced after removal of the medium, and shaken for 15 min to dissolve the violet formazan for absorbance measurement at 490 nm. The cell viability was calculated by the absorbance ratio of experimental cells (with CDs) to control cells (without CDs).

For cell imaging, HeLa cells (7 × 103 per well) were seeded in a 12-well plate and incubated for 24 h. After removal of cell culture medium, CDs solution (1.5 mL, 0.5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 4 h. Subsequently, cells were washed carefully with PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM), and then FeCl3 solution (1.5 mL, 10 μM) was added and incubated for 30 min. Finally, AA (1.5 mL, 100 μM) was added after removal of the medium and incubated for 30 min for cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of dual-emission CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Black pepper was selected as the starting material to fabricate dual-emission CDs. It was reported that black pepper mainly contains hydrophobic alkaloids, porphyrins, fats [25,26], which played an important role in the generation of CDs. In order to effectively extract porphyrins to construct NIR dual-emission CDs, solvothermal method was chosen using ethanol as reaction solvent [14]. Therefore, the ratio of ethanol to black pepper and the reaction time have been optimized in the synthesis procedures. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensities of dual-emission CDs reached the maximum when the mass of black pepper was set at 2.0 g, the volume of ethanol was 40 mL, and the reaction time was 24 h.

TEM image shows that the obtained CDs present a narrow particle size distribution with average diameter at about 4.7 nm (Fig. 2 A). XRD spectrum displays a broad peak at 2θ = 23.5° (Fig. 2B), corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite. FT-IR and XPS characterization were employed to analyze surface groups of CDs. In FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2C), OH and NH stretching vibrations are observed with a broad peak at around 3310 cm−1, CH stretching vibrations are obvious at 2923 and 2852 cm−1, CO, CC, and CN stretching vibrations are found at 1686, 1626, and 1436 cm−1, and CN and CO stretching vibrations can be viewed at 1362 and 1026 cm−1. While in XPS spectrum (Fig. 2D), C, N, and O peaks at 284.76, 399.61 and 530.87 eV are detected in CDs with the relative atomic percentages at 60.8, 12.96 and 26.25%. The high percentage of N might come from the major N containing compounds (e.g. alkaloids, porphyrins) in black pepper. Analysis of the high-resolution spectra of C1s show CC, CN/CC, CO, and CO/CN peaks at 284.27, 285.04, 285.89, and 287.48 eV (Fig. 2E), the high-resolved spectra of N1s present graphitic-N/NH and pyrrolic N peaks at 399.21, and 399.96 eV (Fig. 2F), and the high-resolved O1s spectra indicate the presence of CO and CO peaks at 530.75 and 532.40 eV (Fig. 2G), which are well matched with those in FI-IR.

Optical properties of obtained dual-emission CDs have been analyzed using UV–vis and fluorescence techniques. As shown in UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3 A), CDs have three absorption peaks at 275, 422 and 668 nm. The absorption peak at 275 nm was attributed to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. And two weak absorption peaks at 422 and 668 nm may belong to the n–π* electronic transition of CN and CO of porphyrins. The characteristic absorption peaks of the main alkaloid in black pepper, piperine (254, 310, 342 nm) [26], cannot be found. Alkaloids can be effectively extracted from black pepper by ethanol, which may be completely decomposed and carbonized to form CDs within long-term high temperature refluxing. Fluorescence spectrum of CDs exhibits two emission peaks at 520 nm (green region) and 668 nm (NIR region) under single excitation at 415 nm (Fig. 3A). When the excitation wavelengths increase from 400 to 430 nm, a blue shift excitation-dependent manner is observed for the green emission (Fig. 3B). The excitation-dependent manner is always observed on CDs because of the different particle size and diverse emissive trap sites, and thus, green emission comes from the intrinsic structure of CDs [27]. A red shift excitation-dependent manner is discovered for NIR emission peak at 668 nm during excitation wavelengths increasing from 400 to 415 nm, and excitation-independent happened with excitation wavelengths increasing from 415 to 430 nm. The NIR emission might therefore originate from the surface porphyrins.

In biological imaging, the optical stability of dual-emission CDs is very important. Therefore, the optical stability of CDs was studied at different conditions (e.g. storage period, UV light irradiation, and pH). It can be seen from Fig. S2, fluorescence intensities of CDs at 520 and 668 nm remain almost unchanged under storage at 4 °C for 24 weeks, UV light irradiation at 365 nm for 90 min, and pH from 1 to 9. Therefore, CDs have high stability against storage period and environmental acidity, remarkable photostability, and then are suitable for bioimaging applications.

The quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs was calculated to be 10.25% using quinine sulfate as reference (quantum yield 54% in 0.05 M H2SO4) [28]. The QY is a little higher than CDs using natural products as raw materials (usually less than 10%), which may come from the N atoms doping with high quantities [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of off-on ratiometric AA sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The resultant dual-emission CDs are designed as the NIR fluorescence sensor for off-on ratiometric determination and imaging of AA through Fe3+ mediation. Porphyrins can chelate with Fe3+ to form complex, therefore, the NIR emission of CDs at 668 nm might be quenched by Fe3+. Simultaneously, AA can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ [4], and then if Fe2+ cannot complex with porphyrins, NIR emission of CDs at 668 nm can be recovered through Fe3+ mediation of fluorescence signal.

Firstly, the fluorescence response of dual-emission CDs to metal ions bas been measured. As shown in Fig. S3A, among sixteen common metal ions, value of F668/F520 after addition of Fe3+ was greatly decreased, whereas for other metal ions, the change of fluorescence intensity ratios can be ignored. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs for NIR emission at 668 nm gradually and effectively decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentrations, and the green emission at 520 nm had a slight change (Fig. S3B). The mechanism of Fe3+ quenching the NIR emission of CDs was then investigated systematically. As presented in Fig. S4A, there is an overlap between UV–vis spectrum of Fe3+ and the excitation spectrum of CDs, which can cause inner filter effect (IFE). IFE comes from the absorption of excitation or emission of CDs by quencher, and then the fluorescence of CDs decreases. In contrast, there is no overlap between UV–vis spectrum of Fe3+ and emissive spectrum of CDs, indicating that fluorescence resonance energy transfer is excluded. IFE effect between Fe3+ and CDs has been calculated by the equation presented in Supplementary materials. After removing the fluorescence quenched by IFE, corrected fluorescence and observed fluorescence have no significant difference (Fig. S4B). As a result, only about 10% of the quenched fluorescence between CDs and Fe3+ is attributed to IFE, which is why the green emission at 520 nm also has a slight decrease after addition of Fe3+. Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption peak at 668 nm for porphyrins on CDs red shifted to 688 nm after addition of Fe3+, suggesting the formation of complex between CDs and Fe3+ (Fig. S4C). Kq of CDs with Fe3+ is 2.76 × 1010 M−1 s−1, greater than 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1. Moreover, fluorescence lifetimes of CDs before and after addition of Fe3+ are almost the same (4.533 and 4.532 ns) (Fig. S5). These indicates that fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe3+ comes from SQE by the formation of nonfluorescent CDs-Fe3+ complex. Thus, synergetic effects (SQE and IFE) happened during Fe3+ quenching the NIR emission of CDs, and the outstanding selectivity of CDs to Fe3+ is ascribed to their strong and specific binding affinity.

After addition of AA, UV–vis spectrum recovered, and showed almost the same with that of pure CDs (Fig. S4C). And as expected, fluorescence spectra of CDs-Fe3+ recovered after the introduction of AA (Fig. 4 A). That might be the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ by exposure to AA. In order to testify the redox reaction, 1,10-phenanthroline was added in to CDs-Fe3+ solution, and after introduction of AA, the solution color changed obviously and an obvious UV–vis absorption peak at 514 nm for 1,10-phenanthroline-Fe3+ complex appeared (Fig. S6). That is to say, the fluorescence recovery is attributed to the reduction of Fe3+, and the product Fe2+ is insensitive to CDs (Fig. S3A). Furthermore, small biomolecules were selected to investigation the selectivity to AA, and they did not induce any increase of F668/F520 value of CDs-Fe3+ system (Fig. S7). Therefore, the developed strategy presented excellent selectivity for AA over potent competitive interferences.

The detection parameters have a great influence on the linear range and sensitivity. When Fe3+ concentration was set at 3 μM, CDs concentration (0.5 mg/mL), pH (7.4), incubation time for the formation of CDs-Fe3+ complex (15 min), and incubation time for redox reaction (20 min) have been investigated and optimized (Fig. S8). Then CDs-Fe3+ probe for ratiometric determination of AA has been studied. As depicted in Fig. 4A, NIR emission at 668 nm gradually and greatly enhances with the increase of AA concentration, and the green emission at 520 nm presentes a slight enhancement. An excellent linearity exhibits between F668/F520 (y) and AA concentration (x) in the range of 0.5–50.0 μM (y = 0.075x + 2.06, R2 = 0.997) (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated to be 0.30 µM (3 σ/k) and 0.99 µM (10 σ/k). By Comparison with reported fluorescence sensors for ratiometric detection of AA, black pepper-based CDs have the advantages of simple preparation, high sensitivity, and NIR sensing (Table S1).

Human serum samples were then selected to evaluate the practicality of the prepared CDs. 10-Fold diluted human serum samples were separately spiked with 5, 20, 40 μM of AA, CDs-Fe3+, and then measured. As shown in Table 1 , satisfactory percentage recoveries from 94.6% to 106.5% with a low relative standard deviation (RSD) below 4.6% were carried out. It indicated that the proposed off-on sensing platform can be successfully applied as a fluorescence sensor for AA monitoring with high accuracy and precision in biological samples.

Before being used for cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of CDs was measured using MTT assay. After being incubated with 1.0 mg/mL of CDs for 24 h, HeLa cells still maintained a survival rate of more than 95% (Fig. S9). Results indicated the low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility of CDs. Then CDs were applied to verify the ability to imaging AA in living cells. As shown in Fig. 5 , HeLa cells can be imaged by CDs (0.5 mg/mL) with green and red channels (Fig. 5 A1-A3), indicating the potential of CDs for cellular imaging. After incubation with Fe3+, fluorescence for red channel was almost diminished, while the green channel showed negligible change (Fig. 5 B1-B3), which was similar with those in vitro results in Fig. S3B. Expectedly, after being treated with exogenous AA, fluorescence for red channel was restored, and green channel unchanged (Fig. 5 C1-C3). Therefore, prepared CDs have great potential for intracellular AA determination based on ratiometric fluorescence images.

Novel NIR ratiometric fluorescence CDs have been successfully fabricated using black pepper as raw materials by solvothermal method. The resultant CDs present two emissions at 520 and 668 nm under 415 nm excitation. NIR emission at 668 nm derived from surface porphyrins can be selectively and greatly quenched by Fe3+ because of the formation of non-fluorescent CDs-Fe3+ complex, and then the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ by exposure to AA can recover the fluorescence. Green emission at 520 nm shows negligible change. Then, the prepared CDs have been successfully employed to ratiometrically detect and image AA in living cells with excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, low toxicity, and favorable biocompatibility. The NIR ratiometic detection mode can eliminate the effects of interferences, and presented higher accuracy in biomedical detection and imaging. It is anticipated that the established strategy can provide a new platform in AA tracking for clinical diagnosis.

Cui Tang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Ruiqing Long: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xia Tong: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Ying Guo: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Chaoying Tong: Formal analysis, Investigation. Shuyun Shi: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (2018JJ1043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106000.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-enzymatic simultaneous detection of acetylcholine and ascorbic acid using ZnO·CuO nanoleaves: real sample analysis
Microchem. J.

Clin. Exp. Emerg. Med.

Food Chem.

Anal. Chem.

Food Chem.

Food Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A label-free ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe for ascorbic acid based on redox-modulated dual-emission signals
Analyst
